symptom: DNS not resolving to expected/correct IP address for AMPLS endpoints; private endpoints resolving to public IP or wrong IP address
rootCause: Stale Private DNS Zone entries preventing proper name resolution after AMPLS configuration; DNS zone records may not reflect current private endpoint IPs. Can be client-specific or affect all clients depending on DNS configuration.
solution: Validate DNS connectivity and check for stale Private DNS Zone records. For Application Insights specifically, check for Stale Private DNS Zone preventing proper name resolutions. Collect: (1) Is issue client-specific or affects all clients? (2) What endpoint URL fails to resolve? (3) What IP is being returned vs. what IP is expected? (4) What does the AMPLS resource show for the address?
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/Support Topics/I receive no data or cannot run queries/DNS not resolving to correct IP address

symptom: Application Insights telemetry not being ingested through AMPLS; ingestion fails or errors when running queries when application is hosted on App Service with AMPLS configured
rootCause: App Service free tier does not support VNet integration. The application resolves ingestion endpoints to a public IP instead of the private AMPLS endpoint, causing ingestion failure when AMPLS is configured in private-only mode.
solution: Ensure the web app or function app hosting the application is on a paid App Service plan that supports VNet integration. The free App Service plan does NOT support VNet and cannot use AMPLS for private ingestion. Upgrade to at least a Basic or Standard plan to enable VNet integration.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/Support Topics/I receive no data or cannot run queries/Errors when running queries, or other issue accessing data

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/Support Topics/I receive no data or cannot run queries/No data in Log Analytics, Metrics, or Application Insights

symptom: Cannot add Action Group directly to Built-In Azure Monitor Alerts (Azure Backup, Site Recovery, Stack Edge, Stack HCI, Data Box Gateway, SCOM Managed Instance, Azure Local); action group attachment option missing or not working
rootCause: Built-In Azure Monitor Alerts bypass the Alerts Data Plane and have no resource ID. Action Groups require a resource ID to attach directly. The alerting logic is owned by the onboarded Azure service's PG team, not Azure Monitor.
solution: Use an Alert Processing Rule instead of adding an Action Group directly to Built-In Azure Monitor Alerts. Alert Processing Rules can match and route Built-In alerts to Action Groups without requiring a resource ID on the alert rule itself.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Built-In Azure Monitor Alerts

symptom: Azure Event Grid Built-In Monitor Alerts configuration issues; unclear whether Action Group or Alert Processing Rule is required; confusion about supported resource providers
rootCause: Event Grid Built-In Alerts create an Event Subscription which has a resource ID (unlike other Built-In Alerts). Currently in public preview supporting only Azure VMs and Azure Key Vault as source resource providers. Event Grid processes data from other resource providers before invoking Azure Monitor.
solution: For Event Grid Built-In Alerts: Action Group can be directly added to the Event Subscription (no Alert Processing Rule needed) because the Event Subscription has a resource ID. If troubleshooting data issues, engage Event Grid team first using Jarvis DGrep Logs. Note: only Azure VMs and Azure Key Vault are supported while in public preview.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Built-In Azure Monitor Alerts

symptom: Log Search Alert fires false alerts or misses alerts due to late-arriving log data; ingestion delays cause evaluation window to miss data that arrives after the evaluation runs
rootCause: Late-arriving data: ingestion delay of logs to Kusto causes the evaluation window to miss data. The standard single-window query cannot compensate for variable ingestion latency. Retries also increase load on Draft/Kusto.
solution: Enable Log Alerts optimization flow with sliding window method: the system modifies the customer query to cover multiple time windows (up to 30 minutes back) in a single execution. Query is prepended with LSA_ variables covering 7 windows and appended with a summarize clause. Each result may appear in multiple windows but fires only once per bucket (for both stateful and stateless rules). 30 minutes is the cutoff after which LSA no longer retries.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Log Alerts optimization flow and query manipulation

symptom: Log Search Alert rule not working after assigning managed identity RBAC permissions to workspace and resources; alert still fails evaluation or returns permission errors despite RBAC being configured
rootCause: Assigning RBAC access (Reader role) to the managed identity on workspace/resources is necessary but not sufficient. The managed identity must also be explicitly attached to the log search alert rule configuration itself. Simply assigning RBAC without attaching the identity to the rule has no effect.
solution: Edit the alert rule via Azure Portal/PowerShell/ARM/CLI/API to attach the managed identity to the alert rule in addition to granting RBAC access. This attachment step is required when switching from default identity to managed identity after alert rule creation. The two steps (RBAC grant + rule attachment) must both be completed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Log Data Access for Log Search Alert Rules

symptom: Log Search Alert fails with cross-tenant data access errors; query cannot retrieve log data from resources or workspaces in a different Azure tenant
rootCause: Cross-tenant queries for Log Search Alert rules are not supported as of March 15, 2024, unless the alert rule identity has RBAC roles assigned across tenants using Azure Lighthouse.
solution: Use Azure Lighthouse to assign cross-tenant RBAC roles to the alert rule identity. Alternatively, redesign the alert scope to avoid cross-tenant queries. Reference announcement: 'Sending a log search alert with cross tenant target resource will no longer be supported'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Log Data Access for Log Search Alert Rules

symptom: Log Search Alert rule using linked storage account fails; system-assigned managed identity does not work for alert rules with linked storage; 'Contributor' role on storage is not sufficient
rootCause: Log search alert rules using linked storage require user-assigned managed identity (not system-assigned). The identity requires Storage Blob Data Contributor role (not just Contributor) because alert rules perform write operations on the storage account to store log queries.
solution: (1) Switch to user-assigned managed identity for alert rules using linked storage. (2) Grant Storage Blob Data Contributor role on the linked storage account — Contributor role alone is insufficient. This requirement applies from July 2024 onwards per Azure announcement.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Log Data Access for Log Search Alert Rules

symptom: Log Search Alert query fails with 403 Forbidden / InsufficientAccessError / NspValidationFailedError; alert rule cannot access Log Analytics workspace that is inside a Network Security Perimeter
rootCause: Log Analytics workspace is part of a Network Security Perimeter (NSP) but the log search alert rule is not included in the NSP. NSP denies query access from resources not in the perimeter. Resource-centric alert rules will succeed in evaluation but NSP-protected workspace data will be silently hidden.
solution: Add the log search alert rule to the NSP, OR configure NSP inbound rules to whitelist Alerts and Action Groups IP addresses. Reference: 'Configure Azure Monitor with Network Security Perimeter' documentation. Note: resource-centric rules not in NSP will silently return no data rather than failing.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Log Data Access for Log Search Alert Rules

symptom: Resource-centric Log Search Alert or alert configured with _ResourceId column returns no data despite data existing in the workspace and identity having reader access on the resource
rootCause: Log Analytics workspace configured with 'Require workspace permissions' access control mode. In this mode, even resource-centric alert rules require explicit workspace-level permissions; data from the workspace is hidden if the identity lacks workspace reader role.
solution: Check workspace Access Control Mode in Azure Support Center under workspace properties. Ensure the alert rule identity has Reader role or equivalent permissions on all Log Analytics workspaces queried. Options: (1) Grant workspace reader role to the alert rule identity, or (2) change workspace to 'Use resource or workspace permissions' mode.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Log Data Access for Log Search Alert Rules

symptom: Resource Health shows 'Unavailable' (NO_REASON event) for a Log Search Alert rule; alert runs every 30 minutes or every hour; no actual firing issues observed
rootCause: Alert rules running less frequently than every 15 minutes (30 min, 1 hour, etc.) do not provide Resource Health status updates. The 'unavailable' status is expected behavior for low-frequency alert rules, not an indicator of an actual problem.
solution: This is expected behavior — no action needed if the alert is evaluating and firing correctly. Resource Health only provides status updates for alert rules with frequency ≤ 15 minutes. Verify there are no actual evaluation failures in the alert evaluation history before escalating.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Resource Health for Log Search Alerts

symptom: Log Search Alert Resource Health shows 'Query validation error' (DRAFT_QUERY_VALIDATION); alert rule consistently fails to evaluate and create/edit may also fail
rootCause: Alert rule is targeting a table on the Basic log data plan. Basic logs are not supported in Log Search Alert rules — only the Analytics log data plan is supported for alerting.
solution: Change the log data plan for the target table from Basic to Analytics. Reference: 'Compare the basic and the analytics log data plans' in Azure Monitor documentation. Note: Basic logs offer lower cost but do not support alerting or interactive queries.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Resource Health for Log Search Alerts

symptom: Log Search Alert Resource Health shows 'Workspace not found' (DRAFT_WORKSPACE_NOT_FOUND) or 'Application Insights resource not found' (DRAFT_APPLICATION_NOT_FOUND); alert stops evaluating
rootCause: The alert scope resource (Log Analytics workspace or Application Insights) was moved, renamed, or deleted. The alert rule still references the old or invalid resource path and cannot resolve the target.
solution: Recreate the alert rule with a valid target Log Analytics workspace or Application Insights resource. The original alert rule cannot be updated in-place to redirect to the new resource location.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Resource Health for Log Search Alerts

symptom: Log Search Alert Resource Health shows 'Unauthorized to run query' (DRAFT_UNAUTHORIZED or DRAFT_FORBIDDEN); alert evaluation consistently fails with permission errors
rootCause: Alert rule identity (default identity snapshot user, system-assigned, or user-assigned managed identity) lacks Reader role or equivalent permissions on the queried Log Analytics workspace or resources.
solution: (1) Check and grant Reader role on the Log Analytics workspace and all queried resources. (2) Ensure managed identity is attached to the alert rule (not just granted RBAC). (3) Check evaluation history for specific error messages. Reference: 'Create a log alert rule - managed identity' documentation for managed identity attachment steps.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Resource Health for Log Search Alerts

symptom: Log Search Alert Resource Health shows 'Active alerts limit exceeded' (LIMIT_DAILY_ALERT_COUNT_EXCEEDED) or 'Dimension combinations limit exceeded' (LIMIT_DIMENSION_COMBINATION_COUNT_EXCEEDED); alert evaluation fails
rootCause: (1) Alert rule exceeded the limit of 6000 fired (non-resolved) alerts per alert rule at a time (technical limit: 12000 within 48 hours). (2) Alert rule exceeded dimension combination limits: 6000 for stateless alerts, 300 for stateful alerts.
solution: For alert count limit: Resolve/dismiss existing fired alerts to bring active count below 6000. For dimension limit: Reduce dimension combinations in alert rule configuration (e.g., narrow the split-by fields or add filters). Reference: 'Azure Monitor Service Limits - Alerts' documentation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Concepts/Resource Health for Log Search Alerts

symptom: Client resolves Azure Monitor endpoint FQDN to an incorrect private IP address (different from the expected IP defined in the AMPLS resource)
rootCause: Multiple AMPLS resources on the same VNet or peered VNets create conflicting private DNS zone records, violating the 'single AMPLS per VNet' guiding principle
solution: 1) In ASC, navigate to the App Insights/LA component Properties > Private Links to find the associated AMPLS resource. 2) Navigate to the Private Endpoint to check Private DNS Zone Record Sets for the correct private IP. 3) If the incorrect IP belongs to another AMPLS in the same subscription, consolidate to a single AMPLS resource per the guiding principle. 4) If cross-subscription, escalate to Azure Networking with the correct/incorrect IP data points.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Investigate incorrect private IP resolution for an AMPLS Azure Monitor resource

symptom: Resource resolves Azure Monitor endpoint FQDN to a public IP address instead of the expected private IP address within a private-link-enabled VNet
rootCause: The VNet is not using the Private DNS zones established by AMPLS and the Private Endpoint — either Private Endpoint DNS integration is disabled, or the client resource is not integrated into the VNet with AMPLS
solution: Ensure AMPLS is tied to a Private Endpoint on the same VNet as the client. During PE creation, confirm 'Integrate with private DNS zone' = Yes to auto-register private DNS zones. Run nslookup on the client to confirm the FQDN resolves to the private IP shown in the PE's DNS record sets.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Validate Known Good AMPLS configuration

symptom: Resource resolves Azure Monitor endpoint FQDN to a private IP but not the expected one (IP mismatch between nslookup result and AMPLS-defined IP)
rootCause: Another AMPLS resource was added to the same VNet or a peered VNet, creating duplicate/conflicting Private DNS zone records for Azure Monitor endpoints
solution: Follow guiding principle 'Avoid DNS overrides by using a single AMPLS'. In ASC, check App Insights component Properties > Private Links to identify all associated AMPLS resources. Remove the conflicting AMPLS so only one AMPLS covers each VNet. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/private-link-design#guiding-principle-avoid-dns-overrides-by-using-a-single-ampls
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Validate Known Good AMPLS configuration

symptom: AMPLS Private Link 配置完成后，Web App / Function App 中对 Application Insights 端点（dc.services.visualstudio.com / rt.services.visualstudio.com）的 nslookup 仍解析到公网 IP，而非私有 IP

symptom: Azure Monitor resource endpoint (Application Insights/Log Analytics) resolves to an incorrect private IP address; client is not reaching the expected AMPLS-defined private endpoint IP despite VNet integration being configured
rootCause: Multiple AMPLS resources exist on the same VNet or peered VNets, violating the single-AMPLS guiding principle. DNS override occurs when another AMPLS resource on a peered network defines a different private IP for the same Azure Monitor endpoint FQDN
solution: 1. In ASC, check the Azure Monitor resource Properties > Private Links section to identify the associated AMPLS resource. 2. Navigate to the AMPLS resource (microsoft.insights/privateLinkScopes) and open the Private Endpoint properties to find the expected private IP for the FQDN. 3. Search for other AMPLS resources in the subscription that may define conflicting IP records for the same FQDN. 4. If not found in the same subscription, ask customer about other subscriptions/peered VNets with AMPLS resources. 5. Apply the single AMPLS guiding principle: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/private-link-design#guiding-principle-avoid-dns-overrides-by-using-a-single-ampls. 6. If unresolved, escalate to Azure Networking with: AMPLS resource ID, Azure Monitor resource ID, FQDN, expected vs actual private IPs, Private Endpoint ID, VNet name/ID
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Investigate incorrect private IP resolution for an AMPLS Azure Monitor resource

symptom: Azure Monitor data ingestion fails from a resource (web app/function app) within a VNet configured with AMPLS; nslookup on the Azure Monitor ingestion endpoint returns a public IP address instead of a private IP
rootCause: The resource is not using the Private DNS zones established by AMPLS and the Private Endpoint within the VNet. The resource either lacks VNet integration or the Private DNS zones are not linked to the VNet, causing DNS resolution to fall back to public Azure DNS
solution: 1. Run nslookup from the resource to confirm it returns a public IP. 2. Verify the resource has VNet integration enabled pointing to the VNet containing the AMPLS-associated Private Endpoint. 3. Verify Private DNS zones created by AMPLS + Private Endpoint are linked to the resource's VNet. 4. In ASC, find the Azure Monitor resource > Properties > Private Links to confirm the AMPLS resource association. 5. On the AMPLS resource, confirm the Private Endpoint is on the same VNet as the resource. 6. For Windows App Services, use nameresolver.exe instead of nslookup to check DNS resolution from Kudu console.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Validate Known Good AMPLS configuration

symptom: Azure Monitor data ingestion fails; nslookup on the Azure Monitor endpoint returns a private IP but it does not match the private IP shown in the AMPLS resource's Private Endpoint configuration
rootCause: A second AMPLS resource was added to the VNet or a peered VNet. AMPLS only allows one instance per VNet; adding a second AMPLS causes clients to resolve the Azure Monitor FQDN to the private IP defined by the wrong (conflicting) AMPLS resource
solution: 1. In ASC, find the Azure Monitor resource > Properties > Private Links to get the correct AMPLS and its Private Endpoint. 2. On the Private Endpoint properties, note the expected private IP for the Azure Monitor FQDN. 3. Compare the nslookup result with the expected IP. 4. Search all subscriptions in the network topology for other AMPLS resources that may be defining conflicting DNS records. 5. Consolidate to a single AMPLS per the guiding principle: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/private-link-design#guiding-principle-avoid-dns-overrides-by-using-a-single-ampls. 6. Remove or reconfigure the conflicting AMPLS resource.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Validate Known Good AMPLS configuration

symptom: Need to select correct D365 root cause for AMPLS support cases; reference for L3/L4 root cause classification options for Azure Monitor Private Link Scope issues
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-a-root-cause-an-ampls-issue.md

symptom: App/resource on VNet resolves AMPLS-monitored Azure Monitor endpoint FQDN (e.g. App Insights ingestion endpoint) to a public IP address instead of expected private IP
rootCause: Resource not using Private DNS zones within the VNet; AMPLS Private Endpoint DNS integration ('Integrate with private DNS zone') not properly configured, or Private Endpoint not on the same VNet as the client resource
solution: Verify AMPLS Private Endpoint is attached to the same VNet as the client resource; ensure 'Integrate with private DNS zone' is enabled during Private Endpoint creation; confirm nslookup from client returns a private IP matching the AMPLS PE config (not a public IP); use ASC to navigate AI/LA component > Properties > Private Links to identify the associated AMPLS and PE details
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Validate Known Good AMPLS configuration

symptom: nslookup returns a private IP for an Azure Monitor endpoint but not the expected private IP defined in AMPLS configuration; telemetry or log data fails to reach the endpoint despite resolving to a private address
rootCause: Multiple AMPLS resources exist on the same VNet or peered VNets, causing DNS override conflicts; violates the 'single AMPLS per VNet' guiding principle — the incorrect AMPLS resource elsewhere in the network defines a different private IP for the same endpoint FQDN
solution: Identify the conflicting AMPLS resource: in ASC check all privateLinkScopes in the subscription and peered subscriptions; on each AMPLS check its associated Private Endpoint and 'Private DNS Zone Record Sets' to find which one defines the unexpected IP; remove the extra AMPLS resource or redesign network topology to use a single AMPLS per VNet per the 'Guiding principle: Avoid DNS overrides by using a single AMPLS' (https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/private-link-design#guiding-principle-avoid-dns-overrides-by-using-a-single-ampls)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Validate Known Good AMPLS configuration

symptom: Client attempting to send data to Azure Monitor (App Insights or Log Analytics) resolves endpoint FQDN to an incorrect private IP address — a different IP from what the AMPLS Private Endpoint defines for that endpoint
rootCause: Another AMPLS resource elsewhere in the networked environment (same or peered VNet, possibly different subscription) defines a conflicting private IP for the same Azure Monitor endpoint FQDN, overriding expected DNS resolution
solution: (1) Run nslookup on the FQDN from the client to capture the incorrect IP and the DNS server responding. (2) In ASC find the AI/LA component > Properties > Private Links to identify associated AMPLS; navigate to its Private Endpoint > Properties > 'Private DNS Zone Record Sets' to get the expected private IP. (3) Search the same subscription in ASC for other AMPLS (privateLinkScopes) resources that might define a conflicting IP for the same endpoint. (4) If not found locally, ask customer about AMPLS resources in peered/hub VNet subscriptions. (5) If unresolved, engage Azure Networking with key data: AMPLS scope resource ID, Azure Monitor resource ID, FQDN, expected private IP (from PE DNS records), client-resolved IP (from nslookup), PE resource ID, VNet name and ID
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Investigate incorrect private IP resolution for an AMPLS Azure Monitor resource

symptom: 配置 AMPLS 后，Application Insights 遥测端点（dc.services.visualstudio.com、live.applicationinsights.azure.com）DNS 解析仍返回公网 IP 而非私有 IP，遥测数据未经私有链路传输
rootCause: 以下一项或多项缺失/错误：(1) Spoke VNET 的 Virtual Network Link 未添加到 AMPLs→PE→Private DNS Zone；(2) Spoke VNET 与 Hub VNET 的 VNET Peering 状态非 Connected；(3) Web App/Function App 的 VNET Integration 未配置为路由全部出站流量到 Spoke VNET；(4) AMPLS Ingestion Access Mode 未设为 Private Only
solution: 从 App Service Console/SSH 执行 nslookup dc.services.visualstudio.com 和 nslookup live.applicationinsights.azure.com，验证是否解析到私有 IP。若返回公网 IP，按序检查：(1) AMPLs→PE→Private DNS Zone 是否有 Spoke VNET 的 Virtual Network Link（Linked 状态）；(2) Spoke↔Hub VNET Peering 状态是否为 Connected；(3) Web App VNET Integration 是否使用 Spoke VNET；(4) AMPLS Ingestion Access Mode 是否为 Private Only
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/Learning Resources/Training/Course Materials/AMPLs Setup Guide - Hub and Spoke Networks Private Link Design/Test the Azure Monitor Private Link Connection

symptom: Client resolving AMPLS-connected Azure Monitor resource (Application Insights or Log Analytics) FQDN to incorrect private IP address, causing telemetry ingestion or query failures despite AMPLS configuration appearing in place
rootCause: Multiple AMPLS resources defined on the same VNet or across peered VNets violate the 'single AMPLS per VNet' guiding principle, resulting in DNS override — the client resolves to a private IP defined by a competing AMPLS resource elsewhere in the network topology
solution: 1. Use nslookup (or nameresolver.exe on Windows App Service) from the client to capture the currently resolved IP and DNS server. 2. In ASC, navigate to the AI/LA resource → Properties → Private Links to identify the correct AMPLS resource. 3. Click through to the AMPLS resource in ASC → find the associated Private Endpoint. 4. On the Private Endpoint's Properties under Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints, find 'Private DNS Zone Record Sets' to identify the expected private IP for the FQDN. 5. Compare client-resolved IP vs expected IP — if mismatch, search for other AMPLS resources in the subscription or peered VNets that may be overriding DNS. 6. Remediate by removing duplicate AMPLS resources or restructuring so only one AMPLS exists per VNet. 7. If the competing AMPLS is in a different subscription, engage Azure Networking team with: AMPLS Scope ID, Azure Monitor resource ID, FQDN, expected private IP, actual IP from client, Private Endpoint ID, VNet name and ID.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Investigate incorrect private IP resolution for an AMPLS Azure Monitor resource

symptom: AMPLS Hub-Spoke 配置完成后，从 Web App SSH Console 执行 nslookup 发现 App Insights Ingestion Endpoint（dc.services.visualstudio.com）或 Live Endpoint 仍解析到公网 IP，而非 Private DNS Zone 中配置的私有 IP，导致遥测数据未能通过私有链路传输

symptom: AMPLS-connected client resolves Azure Monitor endpoint FQDN to incorrect private IP address; data not reaching Application Insights or Log Analytics despite AMPLS private link being configured
rootCause: Multiple AMPLS resources exist across peered VNETs causing DNS override conflict; violates 'use single AMPLS per VNET' guiding principle — each VNET only allows one AMPLS instance, so a second AMPLS on a peered VNET causes the wrong private IP to be returned
solution: 1. In ASC Properties page of the Azure Monitor resource (e.g. App Insights Component), find linked AMPLS in the Private Links section. 2. Navigate to the Private Endpoint resource → DNS Zone Record Sets to find the expected private IP. 3. Compare with the IP client is actually resolving to (from nslookup). 4. If conflicting IP found in same subscription, enforce single-AMPLS principle per linked doc. 5. If not found, ask customer about other subscriptions/peered VNETs with AMPLS resources. 6. As last resort, involve Azure Networking with: AMPLS resource ID, Azure Monitor resource ID, FQDN, expected vs actual IPs, PE ID, VNET name/ID.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Investigate incorrect private IP resolution for an AMPLS Azure Monitor resource

symptom: nslookup / nameresolver.exe returns public IP for Azure Monitor ingestion endpoint (Application Insights or Log Analytics) despite AMPLS Private Link being configured; data may not be ingested via private channel
rootCause: The App Service or Function App is not using the Private DNS zones within the VNET that were established by AMPLS and the Private Endpoint — typically VNET integration is not enabled or subnet delegation is missing, so the app bypasses private DNS and resolves to the public endpoint
solution: 1. Run nameresolver.exe (Windows App Service/Function App Kudu → Debug Console → PowerShell) or nslookup on the ingestion endpoint FQDN. 2. If public IP returned, enable VNET Integration on the App Service/Function App pointing to the correct subnet. 3. Confirm Private DNS zones are linked to the VNET. 4. Re-run DNS check to confirm private IP is now returned. 5. To validate known-good: check ASC Properties of App Insights → Private Links → navigate to PE → DNS Zone Record Sets, confirm FQDN resolves to the private IP listed there.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/AMPLS (Azure Monitor Private Link Scope)/How-To/Validate Known Good AMPLS configuration

symptom: Alert fired but Logic App action group connector fails with 'connection removed' error in workflow run history output
rootCause: The API connection for the Logic App connector (e.g., Office 365) has been removed or expired, causing the connector step to fail
solution: Navigate to Logic App Designer, find the failed connector, click 'change connection' at the bottom, and follow the wizard to sign in to the proper account to recreate the connection

symptom: Alert fired but Logic App workflow did not run at all - no execution appears in Logic App Run History, Action Group test returns 404 error
rootCause: The Logic App HTTP trigger URL (Callback URI) has changed or been regenerated, causing a mismatch between the Action Group's stored callback URL and the current Logic App trigger URL
solution: Compare the Callback URL in ASC (Action Group details) with the HTTP URL in the Logic App Designer's first connector ('When an HTTP request is received'). If mismatched, remove the Logic App action from the Action Group and re-add it to get the updated trigger URL

symptom: After May 17/18 2024, users who inherit RBAC roles through Azure AD group membership started receiving unexpected Email Resource Manager Role notification emails from Action Groups or Smart Detection (Failure Anomalies)
rootCause: Design change on May 17-18, 2024: Action Groups with Email Resource Manager Role notification type now send emails to individual users who inherit the specified RBAC role through group-level assignments. Previously, only users with direct role assignments received emails.
solution: This is by-design behavior change. Verify user's RBAC assignment type (direct vs group inheritance) via Azure Support Center → Tenant Explorer → Resource Explorer → Access Control → Check Access. If customer wants to stop emails to group-inherited users, they need to change notification type or adjust RBAC assignments.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Action Groups and Notifications/How to check which users have an ARM role to investigate the notification type Email Resource Manager Role or smart detections emails

symptom: Need to check the delivery status of an Azure Notification (email, SMS, webhook, etc.) - customer reports notification not received or wants to verify notification was sent
rootCause: Notification delivery issues can be caused by rate limiting, invalid recipient, mechanism failure, or upstream alert evaluation issues. NotificationState and FailureReason in AzNS Kusto table reveal the exact cause.
solution: Query AzNSTransmissions_All table in Kusto (cluster: aznscluster.southcentralus.kusto.windows.net, database: AzNSPROD) using NotificationId or SubscriptionId. Check NotificationState (complete=success, failed/skipped=check FailureReason), MechanismType, CompletedTime. KQL: AzNSTransmissions_All | where NotificationId == "<id>" | sort by CreatedTime desc | project-reorder NotificationId, TransmitId, NotificationState, MechanismType, CreatedTime, CompletedTime, FailureReason, AssociatedGroupId, AdditionalInfo
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Action Groups and Notifications/How to get status of an Azure Notification from Kusto

symptom: Need to check delivery status of an Azure Service Health Notification - customer reports not receiving Service Health alerts or wants to trace a specific Service Health event notification
rootCause: Service Health notifications flow through AzNS with UserIdentity SHR_dialtone@activitylogalertsvc.com. Delivery failure reasons visible in NotificationState and FailureReason fields.
solution: Query AzNSTransmissions in Kusto (cluster: aznscluster.southcentralus.kusto.windows.net, database: AzNSPROD) filtered by action group resource ID and UserIdentity =~ SHR_dialtone@activitylogalertsvc.com. Optionally filter by SHAMetadata contains tracking ID. KQL: AzNSTransmissions | where AssociatedGroupId =~ "<actionGroupResourceId>" | where CreatedTime > ago(7d) and UserIdentity =~ "SHR_dialtone@activitylogalertsvc.com" | where SHAMetadata contains "<trackingId>"
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Action Groups and Notifications/How to get status of an Azure Service Health Notification from Kusto

symptom: Email notification from Azure Monitor alert not received or partially delivered - need to trace email delivery through MEO (Microsoft Email Orchestrator, formerly AEO)
rootCause: Email delivery failure through MEO can be caused by SMTP relay rejection, invalid recipient domain, or message processing errors. MessageStatusHistory shows processing status (Added→Generated→Sent) and MessageDelivery shows SMTP response codes.
solution: 1) Get AEO BatchId from AzNS trace logs (search for AEOBATCH in Message property). 2) Query meoshared.westus.kusto.windows.net / AeoAnalytics: use MessageStatusHistory_All_Shared and MessageDelivery_All_Shared tables filtered by BatchId. 3) Check Status: Added=queued, Generated=email created, Sent=forwarded to SMTP. SmtpCode 250=accepted. 4) If AzNS confirms sent to MEO but email not delivered → escalate to MEO via ICM template in ASC (only after confirming AzNS processed successfully).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Action Groups and Notifications/How to get trace logs for emails sent via MEO (Formerly AEO) in Kusto

symptom: Azure Monitor alert notification not received - suspected rate limiting on action group notifications (email, SMS, voice, webhook)
rootCause: Azure Notification Service applies rate limits to prevent notification flooding. When rate limit is reached, notification is suppressed with Need Notify = False flag.
solution: 1) Get NotificationId. 2) Trace notification in ASC or Jarvis. 3) Search diagnostic log results where Message contains "need notify". 4) If matched with "Need Notify - False", rate limiting was applied and target receiver was suppressed. Reference: https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/platform/alerts-rate-limiting
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Action Groups and Notifications/How to identify if rate limits have been reached for an Azure Notification

symptom: Action Group runbook action may be using legacy built-in runbook (Run As Account) instead of updated V2 runbook (Managed Identities) - need to identify which version is in use
rootCause: Action Groups with built-in runbook actions (e.g. RestartAzureVM) were originally created using Run As Accounts. Updated V2 runbooks use Managed Identities. Legacy runbooks may fail when Run As Account is deprecated.
solution: Three identification methods: 1) Check runbook Jobs in Automation Account - if name contains V2 (e.g. RestartAzureVMAlertGlobalRunbookV2), it is the updated version. 2) Use Get-AzAutomationWebhook to list webhooks and match to Action Group JSON view under automationRunbookReceivers. 3) As support engineer: find webhook name in ASC Action Group details, then query OaaS Kusto cluster (oaasprodeu.northeurope.kusto.windows.net / OaaSProd) - query AzureAutomationAuditLogs + EtwAll by webhook name and correlationId. If RunbookName ends with V2, it is updated.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Action Groups and Notifications/Identify if an Action Group runbook action uses a legacy built-in runbook or an updated built-in runbook

symptom: Need to analyze Activity Log Alert evaluation history - want to check if alert rule evaluated correctly and which events triggered or were suppressed
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-activity-log-alert-evaluation-asc.md
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Activity Log Alerts/How to analyze Activity Log Alert event evaluation history in Azure Support Center

symptom: Need to analyze Activity Log Alert event evaluation history in sovereign clouds (Mooncake/Fairfax) where ASC execution history is not yet available
rootCause: Activity log alerts re-platforming is not yet completed in sovereign clouds, so ASC execution history tab is unavailable. Must use Kusto directly.
solution: Query SettingEvaluation table in sovereign Kusto clusters. Mooncake: azureinsightsmc.chinaeast2.kusto.chinacloudapi.cn (database: azureinsightsmc). Fairfax: azureinsightsff.usgovtexas.kusto.usgovcloudapi.net (database: azureinsightsff). KQL: SettingEvaluation | where TIMESTAMP between (StartTime..EndTime) | where isnotempty(ruleTriggered) | where scopeId == alertRuleSubscriptionId | where ruleResourceGroup == alertRuleResourceGroup | where ruleName == alertRuleName | join kind=inner (EventData) on $left.eventId == $right.eventDataId | project TIMESTAMP, scopeId, ruleTriggered, eventId, category, eventTimestamp, operationName, properties
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Activity Log Alerts/How to analyze Activity Log Alert event evaluation history in Kusto for sovereign clouds

symptom: Service Health Alert rules do not fire as expected; alerts configured with invalid or outdated service names via REST API or ARM template fail silently
rootCause: A bug allowed customers to create Service Health alert rules with invalid/outdated service names through API or ARM templates. The portal Services dropdown showed valid names, but API/ARM had no validation, so rules with wrong names never matched incoming events.
solution: PG deployed a fix (rollout started Dec 2023, completed ~Feb 2024): (1) Validation now rejects invalid service names on create via API/ARM; (2) Existing rules with invalid names are automatically mapped to correct names behind the scenes (transparent to user). Customer action: run the provided PowerShell script (Azure Automation with System Managed Identity + Monitoring Reader role) to identify misconfigured rules. Valid service names available via API: GET https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/metadata?api-version=2022-10-01. Customers may start receiving alerts for services they previously didn't (because previously invalid rules are now valid). Tracking ID: JN_B-HSZ.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Activity Log Alerts/How to identify Service Health Alerts configured with invalid service names using PowerShell

rootCause: Bug in alert processing rules allowed filtering on non-common alert schema fields in alert context (payload) filter; fix enforces only common alert schema fields as documented
solution: Migrate alert processing rule filters to use alternative filter types: Platform/Resource Health alerts → use "Resource" filter instead of resourceName/resourceId; Application Insights/Log Alerts v2 → use "Alert rule name" and "Description" filters; Log Analytics v1 → review filter after rollout as format changes

symptom: Log search alerts fire with delay; customer observes 'delay in mins' value in ASC Execution History
rootCause: Log Search Alerts have built-in retry mechanism (30s, 5min, 30min intervals) that re-queries for late-arriving data. Non-optimized flow retries same period; optimized flow uses sliding window model. Maximum retry cutoff is 30 minutes.
solution: 1. Check Execution History in ASC for the fired alert. 2. Find the Operation ID and check Alert latency in Execution Summary. 3. Review Execution Logs for retry details. 4. Check if alert rule is optimized via Properties > Is Optimized field. 5. Late-arriving data (<30 min) is expected to cause delay due to retry mechanism.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to Validate Log Search Alerts fired with delay - Retry mechanism

Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to interpret log alert evaluation failure messages
symptom: Log alert evaluation fails with 403 Forbidden: Given credentials do not have access to read logs (no NSP details)
solution: Customer edits the alert rule (even a simple enable/disable) with user credentials that have read access to query the logs. See https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-monitor/logs/manage-access
rootCause: For non-managed-identity rules, permissions are based on the last user who edited the rule. That user lost or never had read permissions on the target workspace/table.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to interpret log alert evaluation failure messages
symptom: Log alert evaluation fails with 403 Forbidden and NspValidationFailedError: Access denied due to failed Network Security Perimeter validation
solution: Adjust NSP settings: either include the log search alert rule in the perimeter, or whitelist Alerts and Action Groups IP addresses in NSP inbound rules.
rootCause: Log Analytics workspace is configured with NSP that requires resources to be within the perimeter. The log search alert rule is not part of the NSP or no inbound rules allow Alerts to query the workspace.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to interpret log alert evaluation failure messages
symptom: Log alert evaluation fails with 400 BadRequest: Failed to resolve scalar expression named <column>; or column not found in Measure/Dimensions
solution: Ensure the column referenced in Measure/Dimensions is returned by the query, or change the measure/dimension to a field that the query actually returns.
rootCause: Column selected in Measure or Dimensions is not returned by the alert rule query. Rule was created before validation was deployed, or customer used -skipQueryValidation flag.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to interpret log alert evaluation failure messages
symptom: Log alert evaluation fails with 404 NotFound: The workspace could not be found / The application could not be found
solution: Delete the alert rule and create a new one with the updated scope pointing to the correct workspace/AI component.
rootCause: The Log Analytics workspace or Application Insights component that holds the data was moved or deleted.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to interpret log alert evaluation failure messages
symptom: Log alert with managed identity fails with 403 Forbidden or identity-related errors (unexpected response payload, resource does not have access, identity not found)
solution: 1) Grant the managed identity read access on all queried workspaces/ADX clusters. 2) For inconsistency errors, disable/enable the rule. 3) If identity is missing, add a managed identity to the rule. See https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/active-directory/managed-identities-azure-resources/overview
rootCause: Managed identity associated with the alert rule lacks read access on all workspaces/data sources queried, or identity was deleted/detached, or MSI state inconsistency between ARM and LSA.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to unzip a log alert troubleshooting query
symptom: Log alert troubleshooting query in ASC is truncated/partially displayed due to query compression (optimization flow)
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-unzip-log-alert-troubleshooting-query.md

Add ingestion_time() filter to reproduce evaluation-time data: append | where TimeGenerated >= datetime(QueryStartTime) and TimeGenerated <= datetime(QueryEndTime) | where ingestion_time() < datetime(FinalSearchExecutionTime) to the customer query. For join/union queries, add the filter to each table separately. Run in ASC Query Customer Data tab with custom time range.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to validate query execution results on false or missed Log alert
rootCause: Ingestion delay of logs to Kusto causes alert query evaluation against partial data. Results differ because more data arrives after evaluation time.
symptom: Log alert fired when it should not have (false alert) or missed firing (missed alert); re-running customer query returns different results than alert evaluation

solution: Replace bin(TimeGenerated, interval) with bin_at(TimeGenerated, interval, datetime(QueryEndTime)) when validating alert query results to match the exact bucketing used during evaluation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Log Alerts/How to validate query execution results on false or missed Log alert
rootCause: LSA service automatically converts bin() to bin_at() at runtime using query end time as fixed reference point. Manual bin() rounds to nearest interval from epoch, causing different bucket boundaries.
symptom: Log alert query using bin() function produces different time bucketing results when re-running manually vs during alert evaluation

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Metric Alerts/How to get platform metric definition from Kusto
symptom: Need to look up platform metric definition (supported aggregations, FillGapWithZero, MDM namespace) for a metric alert
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-platform-metric-definition-kusto.md

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Metric Alerts/How to identify Red-Black mismatch causing false or missed metric alerts
rootCause: Red-Black mismatch in Geneva Metrics: metric data is not identical on both instances (red/black) of the stamp, causing inconsistent threshold evaluation results.
symptom: Metric alert fires falsely or misses firing; metric value differs between red and black instances of Geneva Metrics stamp
solution: In ASC evaluation logs, add the second instance (-red or -black suffix) to the Datacenter filter, then filter by LogTag in (ThresholdViolated,CombinationEvaluation). Compare ThresholdViolated and metric values between instances. If one shows ThresholdViolated and the other does not → confirmed red-black mismatch (escalate to PG).

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Metric Alerts/How to query metric alert state change history from Kusto
symptom: Need to query metric alert state change history (Active/Resolved) beyond ASC 7-day limit
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-metric-alert-state-change-history-kusto.md

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/How-To/Metric Alerts/How to use the Metric Delay dashboard to identify late arriving data
rootCause: Late arriving metric data: metric ingestion latency exceeded the 3-minute buffer that metric alerts enforce before firing. Alert evaluated against incomplete metric values.
symptom: Metric alert fired when it should not have (false alert); metric value at evaluation time differs from resource metric chart value
solution: Check MetricDelay Jarvis dashboard (https://jarvis-west.dc.ad.msft.net/dashboard/MetricTeamInternalMetrics/Sla/MetricDelay). Set Metrics Backend and Environment, filter by evaluation time. For platform metrics check Metric Delay chart; for custom metrics (CUSTOM account or azmon stamp) check Metric Puller Service absolute publication delay chart. If latency > 180 seconds at metric data window end time → confirmed false alert due to late arriving data.

symptom: Prometheus rule group updates not taking effect after modification - changes made to rule group are not being processed by the data plane
rootCause: Synchronizer component (ModifiedRuleGroupIdsSynchronizerRunner / RuleGroupsSynchronizerProcessor) failed to sync rule group updates from ARM control plane to data plane in Azalertsprodweu cluster
solution: 1) Query AlertRuleTelemtry in Azalertsprodweu/Insights with RuleArmId to get RuleUpdateTimes 2) Check synchronizer processing logs in Application Insights (promalerts-ai-privatepreview) for ModifiedRuleGroupIdsSynchronizerRunner + RuleGroupsSynchronizerProcessor entries 3) If processing logs empty, updates were not synced 4) If error message found, open CRI using "Azure Monitor | Prometheus alerts control plane" escalation template in ASC

symptom: Customer wants to restore deleted alert rules or action groups
rootCause: Most Azure Monitor alert resource types (Log Search Alert, Activity Log Alert, Service Health Alert, Metric Alerts, Smart Alert Rules) cannot be restored once deleted. Only Action Groups support restoration via ICM.
solution: For deleted Action Groups: request restoration via ICM to PG. For deleted alert rules (all types): PG can only provide last known configuration — customer must recreate the rule manually using that info.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Known Issues/Resource Recoverability

symptom: Log alert notification does not contain search results in payload
rootCause: Starting with log alerts API version 2021-08-01, search results were removed from alert notification payload. Only rules created with older API (2018-04-16) still include them.
solution: Use Logic Apps to add search results back to notification. See 'Customize alert notifications by using Logic Apps' in MS Learn docs.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Support Area Paths (SAPs)/Action or notification did not work as expected/Action or notification has an unexpected content

symptom: MetricValue field shows as 'null' in resolved log alert notification
rootCause: By design — stateful log alerts use time-based resolution logic rather than value-based. No metric value caused the alert to resolve; it resolved due to elapsed time.
solution: This is expected behavior. Inform customer that stateful log alerts resolve based on time, not value, so MetricValue will be null in resolved notifications.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Support Area Paths (SAPs)/Action or notification did not work as expected/Action or notification has an unexpected content

symptom: Log alert fired as expected when no results returned, but dimensions list is empty or alert title does not contain dimension name
rootCause: By design — when a query returns 0 rows, the resource ID field (basis for populating dimension and title fields) is empty, resulting in empty dimensions and missing dimension names in the title.
solution: This is expected behavior. When no rows are returned, dimension information cannot be populated. Inform customer this is by-design behavior.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Support Area Paths (SAPs)/Action or notification did not work as expected/Action or notification has an unexpected content

symptom: Custom properties defined in alert rule are missing from email, SMS, or push notifications
rootCause: By design — custom properties are only passed to action payloads (webhook, Azure function, logic app), not to notification channels (email, SMS, push).
solution: Custom properties only work with actions (webhook/Azure Function/Logic App). If customer needs custom data in email, use Logic App with email action instead of direct email notification.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Support Area Paths (SAPs)/Action or notification did not work as expected/Action or notification has an unexpected content

symptom: Azure Monitor alert fired but expected email notification was not received — structured multi-step diagnostic covering APR suppression, Action Group misconfiguration, and recipient-side mail filtering
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-expected-email-not-received-alerts.md
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Support Area Paths (SAPs)/Action or notification did not work as expected/Expected an email, but did not receive it

symptom: Log search alert remains in fired state and does not resolve automatically when the alert condition is no longer met
rootCause: Alert rule not configured as stateful (Automatically resolve alerts not enabled), or alert processing rules suppress resolved condition notifications, or retention period exceeded (max 30 days)
solution: Verify alert is configured as Stateful with "Automatically resolve alerts" enabled in portal (or autoMitigate=true via API). Check no alert processing rules suppress resolved condition notifications on the relevant scope. Note: maximum retention period for alert investigation is 30 days; older alerts cannot be investigated. For stateful log alerts, the alert resolves automatically when the condition is no longer met. Reference TSGs: Troubleshooting Log Search alert didn't resolve, How to check if a Log alert rule is stateful in Kusto, How to get stateful log alert state change history.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Support Area Paths (SAPs)/A fired alert wasn't resolved when it should/Log search alert issue

symptom: Metric alert remains in fired state and does not resolve automatically when the metric no longer meets the threshold
rootCause: Metric time series still meets condition within 3 consecutive evaluation periods; or alert rule defined as stateless (autoMitigate=false / Automatically resolve alerts unchecked); or alert processing rules suppress resolved condition notifications; or customer checking wrong metric time series (wrong resource/dimension)
solution: Metric alert resolves when condition not met for 3 consecutive evaluations (e.g. 15 min for 5-min aggregation granularity). 1) Verify correct metric time series — if rule monitors multiple resources or dimensions, each combination fires/resolves independently. 2) Ensure autoMitigate=true / "Automatically resolve alerts" enabled. 3) Check no alert processing rules suppress resolved condition on the relevant scope. Reference TSG: Troubleshooting Metric alert didn't resolve when it should.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Support Area Paths (SAPs)/A fired alert wasn't resolved when it should/Metric alert issue

symptom: Availability test alert fires unexpectedly (false positive); customer believes the metric conditions did not warrant an alert to fire
rootCause: Mismatch between Azure Portal metric chart settings and alert rule configuration: Aggregation type differs from alert rule, Time granularity differs from Lookback period, or dimensions configured in chart not matching those in the alert rule. Alternatively, autoMitigate=false causes new alerts even with existing unresolved alerts.
solution: 1) In Azure Portal, open the fired alert and review "Why did this alert fire?" section to see Metric Value vs Threshold at fire time. 2) Ensure Aggregation in metric chart matches Aggregation type in alert rule. 3) Ensure Time granularity matches Lookback period (not set to automatic). 4) Verify dimensions in metric chart match those in alert rule. 5) If rule fires new alerts with existing unresolved ones, check autoMitigate setting — if false, alerts won't auto-resolve and new ones keep firing. Availability alerts are metric alerts for Application Insights web test metrics. Reference TSG: Troubleshooting Metric alert fired when it shouldn't have (False Alert).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Support Area Paths (SAPs)/An alert fired when it shouldn't have/Availability test alert issue

symptom: Activity log alert fired with delay; delay observed between event occurrence (step 1) and Activity Log ingestion (step 2)
rootCause: Resource Provider submitted the event into Activity Log (OBO) with delay. Expected delay: up to ~2.5 minutes.
solution: Open a collaboration task with the applicable resource provider support team to investigate the RP-side delay.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Activity log alert fired with delay

symptom: Activity log alert fired with delay; delay between Activity Log ingestion (step 2) and internal LA workspace ingestion (step 3) for non-health events
rootCause: OBO ingestion delay in Activity Logs pipeline. Expected delay: < 1 minute.
solution: Escalate to Activity Logs PG via CRI. Use Azure Monitor | Activity Logs escalation template in ASC. Must provide EventCorrelationId found in ICM description.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Activity log alert fired with delay

symptom: Activity log alert fired with delay; delay between LA workspace data available (step 3) and query availability (step 4)
rootCause: Log Analytics ingestion latency. Expected delay: 30 seconds to 3 minutes.
solution: Escalate to LA ingestion PG via CRI. Use Azure Log Analytics | Ingestion escalation template in ASC. Provide LA workspace details.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Activity log alert fired with delay

symptom: Activity log alert fired with delay; delay between query availability (step 4) and alert sent to AMP (step 5)
rootCause: Activity Log Alerts evaluation engine processing delay. Expected delay: up to 3-4 minutes.
solution: Escalate to Alerts data plane team via CRI. Use Azure Monitor | Activity Log Alerts - Data Plane - False or missed alerts escalation template in ASC.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Activity log alert fired with delay

symptom: Activity log alert fired with delay; delay between AMP receiving alert (step 5) and AMP acknowledgment (step 6)
rootCause: Alerts Management Platform (AMP) component processing delay. Expected delay: ~30 seconds.
solution: Escalate to AMP PG via CRI. Use Azure Monitor | Alert Management Platform (AMP) escalation template in ASC.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Activity log alert fired with delay

solution: Verify Event Hub name, namespace, and connection settings in the action group. Ensure the Event Hub exists and the action group has proper authorization to send messages.

solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-troubleshoot-failure-anomalies-false.md

rootCause: Application failure ratio did not exceed the baseline minimum value determined based on the app request/dependency volume. Detection rejected due to minimum ratio threshold.
symptom: Failure Anomalies (Smart Detection) alert did not fire - detector run log shows minimum ratio rejection
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Failure Anomalies alert didnt fire when it should (Missed Alert)
solution: Check azalertsprodweu/DeepInsights traces for SmartDetectorId=FailureAnomaliesDetector. Look for detection rejected due to minimum ratio message. Compare actual failure ratio against MinRatio threshold. This is expected behavior - no alert should fire.

rootCause: Failure spike rate was below the 3-Sigma threshold (baseline average rate + 3 * standard deviation). For example, spike at 0.079% but threshold is 0.185% (baseline 0.038% + 3*0.049%).
symptom: Failure Anomalies (Smart Detection) alert did not fire - detector run log shows 3-Sigma rejection
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureMonitor/AzureMonitor.wiki:/Alerts/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Failure Anomalies alert didnt fire when it should (Missed Alert)
solution: Check azalertsprodweu/DeepInsights traces for SmartDetectorId=FailureAnomaliesDetector. Look for Rejection Reason = 3-Sigma in DeepInsightResult. Compare DetectionSpikeRate against DetectionBaselineThreshold. This is expected behavior - failure spike not significant enough.

solution: Expected behavior - no anomalies detected in current data. Does not rule out underlying issues. Consider manual investigation of specific metrics and logs.

rootCause: Azure Monitor Investigation depends on OpenAI for generating summaries. OpenAI may experience temporary issues.
solution: Refresh page and run investigation again from scratch. While OpenAI is unavailable, users can still review detected anomalies without the summary.
